title: NET/JRF Mock Test 1: Utility Analysis
instructions: Standard Marking: +1.0 for Correct, -0.25 for Wrong.

questions.questionText: According to the Cardinal Utility Approach, utility is measured in:
questions.explanation: Cardinal utility assumes utility can be quantified into measurable units called 'Utils' (proposed by Marshall).

questions.questionText: The Law of Diminishing Marginal Utility was first formulated by:
questions.explanation: H.H. Gossen first formulated it, hence it is known as Gossen's First Law.

questions.questionText: At the point of consumer equilibrium in cardinal utility (for a single commodity), which condition holds true?
questions.explanation: A consumer maximizes satisfaction when the marginal utility of a good equals its price (MUx = Px).

questions.questionText: If Total Utility (TU) is maximum, Marginal Utility (MU) is:
questions.explanation: When TU reaches its peak (maximum), the addition made by the last unit (MU) is zero.

questions.questionText: The Indifference Curve approach to utility analysis is based on:
questions.explanation: Indifference curve analysis (Hicks and Allen) relies on ordinal utility, meaning consumers can only rank preferences.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is NOT a property of standard indifference curves?
options: Slope downwards from left to right
questions.explanation: Indifference curves never intersect each other. If they did, it would violate the axiom of transitivity.

questions.questionText: The slope of an Indifference Curve is measured by the:
questions.explanation: The slope of an IC is the Marginal Rate of Substitution (MRSxy), which indicates how much of Y is sacrificed for an extra unit of X.

questions.questionText: If two goods are perfect substitutes, their indifference curve will be:
questions.explanation: For perfect substitutes, the Marginal Rate of Substitution is constant, resulting in a straight-line IC.

questions.questionText: If two goods are perfect complements, their indifference curve will be:
questions.explanation: Perfect complements are consumed in fixed proportions (e.g., right and left shoes), making the IC right-angled or L-shaped.

questions.questionText: The slope of the budget line is determined by:
options: The ratio of the prices of two goods
questions.explanation: The slope of the budget line (price line) is Px/Py (the ratio of the prices of good X and good Y).

questions.questionText: At the optimal consumption point, the consumer's indifference curve is:
questions.explanation: Equilibrium occurs where the highest attainable IC is tangent to the budget line, meaning MRSxy = Px/Py.

questions.questionText: The 'Income Effect' for a normal good is:
questions.explanation: For normal goods, an increase in real income leads to an increase in quantity demanded (positive income effect).

questions.questionText: Giffen goods are a special case of:
questions.explanation: All Giffen goods are inferior goods where the negative income effect outweighs the positive substitution effect.

questions.questionText: In the case of a Giffen good, the demand curve is:
questions.explanation: Because the price effect is positive (higher price = higher demand), the demand curve for a Giffen good slopes upward.

questions.questionText: The substitution effect is always:
questions.explanation: The substitution effect is always negative; a fall in relative price always leads to a higher quantity demanded.

questions.questionText: Who introduced the 'Revealed Preference Theory'?
questions.explanation: Paul Samuelson developed the Revealed Preference Theory based on observed consumer behavior in the market.

questions.questionText: The Weak Axiom of Revealed Preference (WARP) implies:
questions.explanation: WARP ensures consistency: if A is revealed preferred to B, then B cannot be revealed preferred to A in the same circumstances.

questions.questionText: According to Hicks, the substitution effect is measured along:
questions.explanation: Hicksian substitution keeps utility constant, moving the consumer along the same indifference curve.

questions.questionText: Slutsky's substitution effect keeps which of the following constant?
questions.explanation: Slutsky compensates the consumer to keep their initial bundle just affordable, maintaining purchasing power (cost difference).

questions.questionText: Consumer Surplus is the difference between:
options: What a consumer is willing to pay and what they actually pay
questions.explanation: Consumer surplus = Maximum Willingness to Pay - Actual Market Price paid.

questions.questionText: The concept of Consumer Surplus was introduced by:
questions.explanation: Alfred Marshall formulated and popularized the concept of Consumer Surplus in utility terms.

questions.questionText: If the demand curve is perfectly elastic, consumer surplus will be:
questions.explanation: For a perfectly elastic (horizontal) demand curve, willingness to pay equals the market price exactly, so surplus is zero.

questions.questionText: Which utility function implies a constant elasticity of substitution?
questions.explanation: Cobb-Douglas (CES=1), Leontief (CES=0), and the general CES function all have constant elasticity of substitution.

questions.questionText: In a Cobb-Douglas utility function U = X^a * Y^b, the income expansion path is:
options: A straight line from the origin
questions.explanation: Cobb-Douglas is a homothetic function, meaning the income expansion path (Engel curve) is a straight line through the origin.

questions.questionText: Engel's Law states that as income increases, the proportion of income spent on food:
questions.explanation: Ernst Engel observed that the budget share dedicated to food falls as a household's income rises (income elasticity < 1).

questions.questionText: For a Veblen good, the demand curve is:
questions.explanation: Veblen goods (status symbols) have a snob appeal; higher prices make them more desirable, leading to an upward-sloping demand.

questions.questionText: The Bandwagon effect makes the market demand curve:
questions.explanation: When consumers buy because others are buying, a price drop increases demand more than usual, making it flatter (more elastic).

questions.questionText: The Snob effect refers to a situation where a consumer:
options: Buys more when others buy more
Buys less when others buy more
questions.explanation: The snob effect is the desire for exclusivity; demand falls if too many other people are consuming the good.

questions.questionText: Which of the following breaks the axiom of non-satiation (more is better)?
questions.explanation: Economic 'Bads' (like pollution or garbage) reduce utility, meaning more is worse, violating non-satiation.

questions.questionText: If the Marginal Utility of good X is negative, it implies that good X is:
questions.explanation: Negative marginal utility means consuming an extra unit decreases total utility, characteristic of a 'bad' or a good consumed beyond satiation.

questions.questionText: Gossen's Second Law is also known as:
questions.explanation: It states consumers maximize utility when the ratio of MU to price is equal across all goods (MUx/Px = MUy/Py).

questions.questionText: Under the Marshallian utility approach, the marginal utility of money is assumed to be:
questions.explanation: Marshall assumed the marginal utility of money remains constant to use it as a measuring rod for utility.

questions.questionText: The Price Consumption Curve (PCC) traces the locus of equilibrium points when:
questions.explanation: PCC shows how the consumer's optimal bundle changes as the price of one good varies, holding income and other prices constant.

questions.questionText: If the Price Consumption Curve (PCC) is horizontal, the price elasticity of demand is:
questions.explanation: A horizontal PCC implies total expenditure on the good remains constant despite price changes, meaning elasticity is exactly 1.

questions.questionText: The Income Consumption Curve (ICC) for two normal goods slopes:
questions.explanation: If both are normal goods, an increase in income leads to increased consumption of both, creating an upward-sloping ICC.

questions.questionText: If good X is an inferior good and Y is a normal good, the ICC bends:
questions.explanation: As income rises, consumption of inferior good X falls while normal good Y rises, bending the curve backward toward the Y-axis.

questions.questionText: The Slutsky equation decomposes a price change into:
questions.explanation: The Slutsky equation mathematically splits the total price effect into the substitution effect and the income effect.

questions.questionText: If the income elasticity of demand is greater than 1, the good is a:
questions.explanation: Luxuries have an income elasticity greater than 1, meaning their demand grows proportionally faster than income.

questions.questionText: Cross elasticity of demand between complementary goods is:
questions.explanation: If the price of a complement (e.g., printers) goes up, the demand for the other (ink) goes down, making it negative.

questions.questionText: For perfect substitutes, the cross elasticity of demand is:
questions.explanation: A slight change in relative price shifts all consumption to the cheaper substitute, implying infinite cross elasticity.

questions.questionText: In the Neumann-Morgenstern utility theory, choices are made under:
questions.explanation: The N-M theory of expected utility explains how individuals make decisions in situations involving probabilities and risk.

questions.questionText: A risk-averse individual's utility function of wealth is:
questions.explanation: Risk aversion implies diminishing marginal utility of wealth, which mathematically corresponds to a concave utility function.

questions.questionText: A risk-neutral individual's utility function of wealth is:
questions.explanation: For a risk-neutral person, the marginal utility of wealth is constant, making the utility function a straight line (linear).

questions.questionText: A risk-loving individual prefers:
options: A certain outcome over a gamble with equal expected value
A gamble over a certain outcome with equal expected value
questions.explanation: A risk lover (convex utility function) prefers the gamble due to the high utility attached to the potential winning tail.

questions.questionText: The certainty equivalent is the guaranteed amount of money that yields the same utility as:
options: The expected value of a gamble
questions.explanation: Certainty Equivalent (CE) is the certain sum that makes the individual indifferent between taking the sum and playing the gamble.

questions.questionText: Risk premium is defined as:
questions.explanation: The risk premium is the amount of expected value a risk-averse person is willing to give up to avoid the risk entirely.

questions.questionText: The 'St. Petersburg Paradox' was resolved by introducing the concept of:
questions.explanation: Daniel Bernoulli resolved it by showing that people maximize Expected Utility (diminishing marginal utility of money), not just Expected Value.

questions.questionText: In expected utility theory, the 'Independence Axiom' implies:
options: Preferences between two lotteries are unchanged if mixed with a third
questions.explanation: The independence axiom states that mixing two lotteries with a third irrelevant lottery doesn't change the preference ranking between the first two.

questions.questionText: The Allais Paradox is a violation of which axiom in Expected Utility Theory?
questions.explanation: Maurice Allais demonstrated that humans systematically violate the Independence Axiom when probabilities approach certainty.

questions.questionText: Prospect Theory, which modifies expected utility to account for loss aversion, was developed by:
questions.explanation: Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky developed Prospect Theory to describe real-world behavioral deviations from expected utility.

questions.questionText: In Quasilinear utility (e.g., U(X,Y) = V(X) + Y), the income effect for good X is:
questions.explanation: Quasilinear preferences exhibit a zero income effect for the non-linear good (X); any extra income is entirely spent on good Y.

questions.questionText: For a Quasilinear utility function, the indifference curves are:
questions.explanation: They are parallel shifts along the axis of the linear good (usually vertically along the Y-axis), meaning MRS depends only on X.

questions.questionText: Which utility function generates linear Engel curves?
questions.explanation: All homothetic utility functions (including C-D, Leontief, CES) generate straight-line rays from the origin as Engel curves.

questions.questionText: The mathematical condition for convexity of an indifference curve is a:
questions.explanation: A diminishing Marginal Rate of Substitution (MRS) ensures the IC bends toward the origin (convex).

questions.questionText: If the Marginal Rate of Substitution (MRSxy) is increasing, the indifference curve is:
questions.explanation: An increasing MRS implies the consumer is willing to give up more Y for X as they get more X, making the curve concave (corner solutions).

questions.questionText: A corner solution in consumer equilibrium occurs when:
options: The consumer buys both goods equally
The consumer buys only one of the goods
questions.explanation: A corner solution happens when the IC is steeper or flatter than the budget line everywhere, so the consumer spends all income on one good.

questions.questionText: Corner solutions are typical when goods are:
questions.explanation: For perfect substitutes, if price ratios don't exactly match the constant MRS, the consumer will buy 100% of the relatively cheaper good.

questions.questionText: Lexicographic preferences violate which standard axiom of consumer choice?
questions.explanation: Lexicographic (dictionary order) preferences cannot be represented by a continuous utility function, violating the Continuity axiom.

questions.questionText: The Strong Axiom of Revealed Preference (SARP) rules out:
questions.explanation: SARP extends WARP to multiple steps, ensuring that choice sequences don't form cycles (e.g., A > B > C > A), ensuring transitivity.

questions.questionText: If a consumer is in equilibrium and the price of good X falls, the new equilibrium lies on a:
questions.explanation: A price fall expands the budget set, allowing the consumer to reach a higher indifference curve and attain more utility.

questions.questionText: In an Edgeworth Box, the set of all Pareto efficient allocations is called the:
questions.explanation: The Contract Curve represents all points where the indifference curves of the two consumers are tangent (Pareto optimal).

questions.questionText: At any point on the contract curve in an exchange economy:
options: MRS of person A > MRS of person B
MRS of person A < MRS of person B
MRS of person A = MRS of person B
questions.explanation: Pareto efficiency in exchange requires the marginal rates of substitution to be identical for both consumers.

questions.questionText: Walras' Law states that:
options: Value of excess demands sums to zero
questions.explanation: Walras' Law implies that if n-1 markets are in equilibrium, the nth market must also be in equilibrium (total excess demand value is zero).

questions.questionText: The First Fundamental Theorem of Welfare Economics states that:
questions.explanation: Under certain conditions, any Walrasian competitive equilibrium results in a Pareto efficient allocation of resources.

questions.questionText: The Second Fundamental Theorem of Welfare Economics implies that:
options: Any Pareto efficient allocation can be achieved via competitive markets given lump-sum transfers
questions.explanation: It separates equity from efficiency, showing you can reach any efficient point by first redistributing initial endowments.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is a limitation of Cardinal Utility analysis?
options: It assumes money has constant marginal utility
questions.explanation: Cardinal utility has several flaws: unmeasurable utils, assumption of constant MU of money, and psychological subjectivity.

questions.questionText: The separation of price effect into income and substitution effects was first introduced mathematically by:
questions.explanation: Eugen Slutsky first published the mathematical decomposition in 1915, though Hicks popularized a similar version later.

questions.questionText: In the Hicksian decomposition, real income is kept constant by adjusting:
questions.explanation: Hicks adjusts nominal money income to ensure the consumer can just touch their original indifference curve (constant utility/real income).

questions.questionText: Compensating Variation is the change in income needed to:
options: Restore the consumer to their original utility level after a price change
Move the consumer to a new utility level before a price change
questions.explanation: Compensating variation measures how much money must be taken/given to return the consumer to their old indifference curve after a change.

questions.questionText: Equivalent Variation measures:
options: Income change at old prices to reach the new utility level
Income change at new prices to reach old utility level
questions.explanation: Equivalent variation asks: At original prices, how much income change is equivalent to the proposed price change in terms of utility impact?

questions.questionText: For a normal good, which is largest for a price decrease?
questions.explanation: For a normal good (price drop), Equivalent Variation > Marshallian Surplus > Compensating Variation.

questions.questionText: If demand is perfectly inelastic, the substitution effect is:
questions.explanation: A vertical (perfectly inelastic) demand curve implies no response to price changes, hence the substitution effect is zero (e.g., Leontief preferences).

questions.questionText: A characteristic of 'Network Externalities' is that utility depends on:
options: The price of the good alone
The number of other people consuming the good
questions.explanation: Network externalities (like phones or social media) mean the product's value increases as more people use it.

questions.questionText: If the Engel curve is downward sloping, the good is:
questions.explanation: A downward-sloping Engel curve indicates that as income increases, the quantity demanded decreases (inferior good).

questions.questionText: According to the ordinal approach, utility functions are unique up to a:
questions.explanation: Ordinal utility rankings remain unchanged under any strictly increasing (positive monotonic) transformation, like squaring or taking logs.

questions.questionText: If U(X,Y) = XY, then V(X,Y) = X^2 * Y^2 represents:
questions.explanation: Since squaring is a positive monotonic transformation for positive values, the underlying IC map and preferences are identical.

questions.questionText: The demand curve derived from the Indifference Curve approach is:
questions.explanation: The standard demand curve derived by pivoting the budget line (maximizing utility subject to budget) is the uncompensated or Marshallian demand.

questions.questionText: The Hicksian demand curve is also known as the:
questions.explanation: It's called compensated because it adjusts income to keep utility constant, isolating only the substitution effect.

questions.questionText: For a normal good, the Hicksian demand curve is ________ than the Marshallian demand curve.
questions.explanation: For normal goods, Marshallian demand includes both substitution and income effects, making it more responsive (flatter) than the Hicksian curve.

questions.questionText: Roy's Identity relates:
options: Indirect utility function to Marshallian demand
questions.explanation: Roy's Identity allows one to derive the Marshallian demand function by differentiating the indirect utility function.

questions.questionText: Shephard's Lemma is used to derive:
questions.explanation: Taking the partial derivative of the expenditure function with respect to a price yields the Hicksian (compensated) demand for that good.

questions.questionText: The Indirect Utility Function shows maximum utility as a function of:
questions.explanation: V(P, M) gives the maximum utility achievable given market prices (P) and the consumer's income (M).

questions.questionText: The Expenditure Function is the inverse of the:
questions.explanation: The expenditure function E(P, U) is mathematically the dual (inverse) of the indirect utility function V(P, M).

questions.questionText: If goods X and Y are complements, their cross-price substitution effect is:
questions.explanation: If they are complements, a rise in the price of Y makes you buy less Y and therefore less X, making the substitution effect term negative.

questions.questionText: The 'Water-Diamond Paradox' highlights the difference between:
questions.explanation: Adam Smith's paradox is solved by noting diamonds have high Marginal Utility (scarcity) but low Total Utility, while water has high TU but low MU.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is NOT an assumption of the Cardinal Utility approach?
questions.explanation: Diminishing MRS is an assumption of the Ordinal (Indifference Curve) approach, not the Cardinal approach.

questions.questionText: In the context of utility, what does 'satiation' mean?
options: The consumer has no money left
questions.explanation: Satiation is the point of bliss where consuming an extra unit yields zero additional satisfaction (MU=0).

questions.questionText: If the income elasticity is negative, the good is:
questions.explanation: A negative income elasticity means demand drops when income rises, defining an inferior good.

questions.questionText: The Marginal Rate of Substitution (MRSxy) mathematically equals:
questions.explanation: The slope of the IC (MRS) is the ratio of the marginal utilities: MUx / MUy.

questions.questionText: At the bliss point in an indifference map:
questions.explanation: The bliss point (or satiation point) is a global maximum of utility; moving in any direction from it reduces utility.

questions.questionText: A consumer spends all income on two goods, X and Y. If MUx/Px > MUy/Py, the consumer should:
options: Buy more X and less Y
Buy more Y and less X
questions.explanation: Because X yields more utility per dollar spent, the consumer should reallocate budget to buy more X until the ratios equalize.

questions.questionText: The concept of 'Asymmetric Information' in economics was pioneered by:
questions.explanation: George Akerlof (Market for Lemons) pioneered the study of asymmetric information where one party knows more than the other.

questions.questionText: Moral Hazard occurs:
questions.explanation: Moral hazard occurs post-contract, when one party alters their behavior (takes more risk) because the other party bears the cost.

questions.questionText: Adverse Selection occurs:
questions.explanation: Adverse selection happens pre-contract, where bad risks are more likely to participate in a market (like sick people buying health insurance).

questions.questionText: Signaling is a market mechanism used to overcome:
questions.explanation: Signaling (e.g., getting a college degree) is a way for the informed party to credibly reveal private information to the uninformed party.

questions.questionText: Which model is associated with oligopoly price rigidity?
questions.explanation: Paul Sweezy's Kinked Demand Curve model explains why prices tend to remain stable (rigid) in oligopolistic markets.

questions.questionText: In the Cournot model of duopoly, firms simultaneously choose:
questions.explanation: In the Cournot model, firms compete by simultaneously choosing their output quantities.

questions.questionText: In the Bertrand model of duopoly, firms simultaneously choose:
questions.explanation: In Bertrand competition, firms compete on price. If products are identical, price is driven down to marginal cost.

questions.questionText: A Nash Equilibrium is a situation where:
options: No player has an incentive to deviate unilaterally
One firm acts as a leader
questions.explanation: Nash Equilibrium occurs when each player is making the best decision possible given the decisions of the other players.

questions.questionText: In the Stackelberg duopoly model, there is a:
questions.explanation: The Stackelberg model is a sequential game where a 'First Mover' (Quantity Leader) sets output before the follower.
